schools.summary: Following the denial-prophecy (13:38), Christ calms hearts (the seat of spiritual life) shaken by his departure and their dashed kingdom-hopes; the remedy is faith.
Augustine: ‘believe in God, believe also in me’ affirms Christ’s deity — the second would not follow unless he were God; the form of God will raise the troubled.
Calvin: he points them where to find courage — by faith — against the arduous contest of seeing him crucified.
‘My Father’s house’ = heaven; ‘mansions’ (monai, abiding places) — Christ’s most precious picture of the heavenly home; he goes to prepare their place (cf. the ark seeking rest, Num 10:33).
Calvin: he goes not to forsake them — there is room for them too; the ‘many’ means room enough, not degrees of honour (against Clarke); Wesley: room for angels and the innumerable multitude.

debates.question: Do the ‘many mansions’ imply degrees of glory?

sides.position: yes — various degrees suited to attainment
no — ‘many’ means room enough for all, not different ranks

schools.summary: ‘I will come again’ read variously (the resurrection, the believer’s death, his spiritual presence, the Parousia) to ‘receive you unto myself’ — the goal being union: ‘where I am, there ye may be also.’
Calvin: chiefly the last day, when he gathers his own, though he dwells in us by the Spirit daily; the crown is everlasting union with Christ.

sides.position: the resurrection and gift of the Spirit
the believer’s death / reception to himself

themes: ‘where I am, there ye may be’

schools.summary: They already knew (or had the means to know) the goal and the way — Christ’s oft-foretold death/resurrection/ascension and his being the way (better reading: ‘ye know the way’); ‘to know our ignorance is the first step.’
Augustine: the mansions existed in predestination, prepared by the purifying of hearts by faith; Calvin: his death is a passage to the Father they too must follow.

themes: death a passage to the Father

schools.summary: Thomas’s literal-minded honesty (cf. 11:16; 20:25) — taking ‘whither’ and ‘way’ materially — voices the disciples’ confusion and brings the difficulty into the open for Christ to resolve.
Calvin and others: the honest ‘we know not’ is the door to instruction; owning his ignorance draws from Christ the clearest self-revelation (v.6).

The threefold claim overwhelms Thomas: Christ is the Way (the only access to the Father, 1 Tim 2:5), the Truth (vs all false religion, the Law’s substance), the Life — answering the disciples’ questions; ‘no man cometh… but by me’ shuts every other door.
Calvin: Christ checks Thomas’s curiosity about heaven for the more necessary subject — how men come to the Father; Origen: the many ‘good things’ the Son is.

themes: ‘I am the way, the truth, and the life’
the only way to the Father

schools.summary: To know Christ truly is to know the Father (they were one, 10:30); the disciples, clinging to temporal-Messiah notions, had not yet fully come to know him, but ‘from henceforth’ are beginning to.
Calvin: he blames the seeking of God by ‘crooked paths’ — we are blind when God stands present before us; Augustine: the way they knew was himself.

themes: knowing Christ is knowing the Father

schools.summary: Philip, catching at ‘seen,’ craves a visible theophany like Moses’ (Exod 33:18) or the Transfiguration — a true desire (the Father would suffice) but a misunderstanding, looking past Christ for it.
Hilary: he wished the Father shown, not with bodily eyes only; Chrysostom: a slowness ‘shameless’ before so plain a truth.
Calvin: the apostles’ fault is our own — we seek God earnestly yet are blind when he stands present before us.
A sorrowful rebuke (three years and not recognised) and a claim to deity: ‘he that hath seen me hath seen the Father’ — words no creature could say (Clarke); the Father revealed in the Son.
Calvin and others: the sight Philip craved has been before him all along — the Father is seen in the Son, the image of the invisible God (1:18).

themes: ‘he that hath seen me hath seen the Father’
the Father revealed in the Son

schools.summary: The mutual indwelling — ‘I in the Father, the Father in me’ — grounds seeing the Father in the Son; proven by his words (not self-originated but the Father’s) and works (the indwelling Father’s).
Augustine: the co-inherence of the divine persons — distinct yet each in the other, one God; Calvin: the words and works are the visible evidence of the hidden unity.

themes: ‘I in the Father, and the Father in me’
words and works of the Father
ground of seeing the Father in the Son

schools.summary: Two grounds of faith: believe his word of the mutual indwelling, or, if that be too high, ‘for the very works’ sake’ — the miracles God’s seal on his claim (cf. 10:38), a ladder for a weak faith.

The believer (chiefly the apostles), one with the Son, becomes God’s instrument; ‘greater works’ read as greater in quantity (Pentecost, Paul) — possible ‘because I go to the Father’ to send the Spirit.
Augustine/Wesley: the ‘greater’ work is the conversion of souls — greater than healing by shadow or handkerchief; Chrysostom: he gives others the power to work.
Prayer’s width (‘whatsoever’) and limitation (‘in my name’ = as his representatives, in his will, not a formula); the Son himself answers (‘that will I do’), to the Father’s glory.
Calvin: the verse implies Christ’s Mediatorship — he is the Author of all done by the apostles, in whose name men pray to the Father.

themes: ‘whatsoever ye shall ask in my name’

schools.summary: A strengthening repetition (emphatic ‘I will do it’); the well-attested ‘ask me’ teaches direct prayer to Jesus (Acts 7:59; Rev 22:20) — the Son both prayed to and answering.
Calvin: no useless repetition — against rash approach and false expedients, Christ holds out the one Mediator by whom God is approached.
Love to Christ is proved by obedience, not feeling or profession (1 John 2:5); ‘continued love prevents disobedience’; Chrysostom: to love him is to keep his commandments, not merely to grieve.
Calvin: their love, though sincere, had superstition mixed (wishing to keep him in the world) — he redirects it to the work he gave; obedient love is the channel of the Spirit.

themes: ‘if ye love me, keep my commandments’
love the channel of the Spirit

schools.summary: Christ as Mediator prays the Father for ‘another Comforter’ (paraklētos, called alongside; allos = another of the same kind as Christ) who, unlike his departing bodily presence, ‘abides for ever.’
Calvin and others: the answer to their grief — a perpetual, universal presence by the Spirit replacing the temporary bodily one, sent at the Son’s asking and the Father’s gift.
‘The Spirit of truth’ (Christ’s own and the Father’s — Bede’s filioque) the world ‘cannot receive,’ lacking spiritual perception; the disciples know him — ‘with you’ now, ‘in you’ at Pentecost.
Calvin: the world’s incapacity is of will as well as nature — it loves darkness; the disciples know him because grace has opened their eyes; Gregory: the Spirit kindles desire of the invisible.

themes: world vs. church at the Spirit

schools.summary: ‘Orphans’ (not ‘comfortless’) — Christ a Father to his ‘little children’ (13:33) will not leave them fatherless but ‘come to you’ (in resurrection, Spirit, abiding presence).
Augustine: the Spirit is not given ‘in place of’ Christ — he comes himself; Hilary: present in his own by the Sacrament (6:56).
Calvin: without the Spirit’s protection men are orphans, helpless — the remedy is Christ governing us by his Spirit.

The world loses sight of him at the cross; the disciples see him by faith and the Spirit (the forty days a step toward spiritual presence); ‘because I live, ye shall live’ is the guarantee of eternal life, the surety of a better covenant (Heb 7:22).
Calvin: the continued seeing is by the eyes of faith, not sense; Clarke: his resurrection is the proof and pledge of ours, his intercession the spring of our life.

themes: world sees no more, ye see me

schools.summary: ‘That day’ = the new era of resurrection and Pentecost (Acts 1-3); then they shall know the threefold union — the Son in the Father (10:38), the believers in the Son, the Son in them.
Calvin: ‘that day’ runs from Pentecost to the last resurrection; the mystical union is known not by speculation but by the Spirit’s teaching.

themes: Son in Father, believers in Son

schools.summary: Successive degrees: keeping the commandments (written on the heart) proves love; the loving are loved by Father and Son; Christ ‘will manifest himself’ — a real spiritual Presence to the obedient believer, not the world.
Calvin: love is repeatedly proved by obedience lest we love ‘something else under the name of Christ’ (2 Cor 5:16); the promise is his inward self-disclosure.
‘Judas, not Iscariot’ (= Thaddaeus/Jude, the epistle’s author) asks, from v.21, how Christ can manifest himself to them but not the world — still half-expecting a public Messianic display.
Augustine: he confuses the inward, spiritual abode now promised with the outward, future one perceptible to bodily sight; the kingdom comes by indwelling, not show.
The answer to Judas: the manifestation is inward, to the loving and obedient; the Father and Son ‘come’ now (not at judgment) and ‘make their abode’ (monē, as v.2) in the believer’s heart.
Calvin: God’s love is not second to our piety but its spring; obedience assures us his grace dwells in us; Wesley: a presence beside which justification seems nothing.

themes: the Father and Son make their abode

schools.summary: The obverse of v.23: not loving Christ shows in not keeping his word — which is the Father’s; the world, rejecting it, closes its own channels of communion (cf. 1 Cor 16:22).
Calvin: a steadying warning — believers mixed with scoffers must not be drawn away, but keep to the end the grace received; Gill: outward keeping without love is no keeping.
A pause and transition: Christ has spoken ‘while abiding with you’; he now hands his teaching to the Spirit, who will interpret his words after his departure.
Augustine: Father, Son, and Spirit all indwell the believer — the Trinity dwells together; Calvin: the seed sown now lies hidden, to be quickened by the Spirit, so they need not despair.

The Comforter (the Holy Ghost), sent by the Father ‘in my name’ (and by the Son, 15:26; 20:22), has a twofold office — to teach all things and to ‘bring to remembrance’ Christ’s words: the charter of apostolic inspiration.
Lightfoot: being ‘led into all truth’ was the apostles’ peculiar lot, though every believer is led into all truth needful for himself.

More than the Oriental ‘Shalom’ of farewell: Christ’s own bequest of ‘my peace,’ ‘not as the world giveth’ — not empty wishing or outward calm but the abiding peace of reconciliation with God (Eph 2:15; Lk 2:14).
Chrysostom: he consoles the love and fear uppermost in them; Origen: peace is given before the storm, as a house is built in calm to stand.
Calvin: the world’s peace is mere wished prosperity; Christ’s is inward and lasting, framing the chapter (v.1) against trouble and fear.

Love should rejoice that he goes to the Father’s glory; ‘the Father is greater than I’ speaks of the Son’s state/office as Mediator in the form of a servant, not of his nature (equal, 10:30; Phil 2:6).
Hilary: the giver is greater, but he to whom unity is given is not less — against Arian misuse; Chrysostom: accommodation to the disciples, who knew the Father’s power.
Calvin: the comparison is not of natures but of the Son’s present low estate against the heavenly glory he goes to share.

debates.question: In what sense is the Father ‘greater than I’?

sides.position: in the Son’s state/office/incarnation, not his nature (orthodox)

schools.summary: As with the betrayal (13:19), Christ foretells his departure ‘before it come to pass’ so that its fulfilment may confirm, not shake, the disciples’ faith.
Calvin: faith is established when Christ’s word is verified by the event; the forewarning arms them against the offence of the cross — an act of pastoral love.

‘The prince of this world’ (Satan, 12:31; 16:11) comes through Judas/the arrest, but ‘hath nothing in me’ — no claim, no foothold of sin in the sinless Son; teaching is nearly over, the conflict at hand.
Calvin and others: death and Satan claim sinners through sin, but the sinless Christ owes nothing — he dies freely, the sinless One in the room of sinners.

He goes to the cross not by Satan’s compulsion but to show the world his love for the Father, doing as commanded — willing, obedient death (10:18); ‘Arise, let us go hence’ turns him toward Gethsemane.
Calvin: he advances readily to meet death, not fleeing but going forth; some note the puzzle of chs 15-17 following ‘let us go hence.’

themes: ‘that the world may know I love the Father’
